title: Stability Analysis of Superconductivity in $\textit{P6/mmm}$-LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$ and its Experimental Reproducibility from La-Sc Alloys
authors: Dmitrii V. Semenok, Ivan A. Troyan, Di Zhou, Emil A. Yuzbashyan, Boris L. Altshuler, Viktor V. Struzhkin
abstract: In this work, we analyze the feasibility of room-temperature superconductivity in the lanthanum-scandium hydride $\textit{P6/mmm}$-LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$. We demonstrate that the electron-phonon coupling calculations performed using the $σ$-broadening of the double $δ$-function at the Fermi surface lead to a very strong dependence of $\textit{T$_c$}(σ)$ on the arbitrary $σ$, whereas the tetrahedral method for the electron-phonon interaction is free from this drawback and leads to $\textit{T$_c$}$ > 300 K at 300 GPa in agreement with previous predictions. By analyzing the stability of the metallic state of LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$ at 250-300 GPa, we show that this compound is at the edge of the stability region ($ξ$ = 0.54), similar to $\textit{fcc}$ LaH$_{10}$ at 140-150 GPa. Experimental attempts to synthesize LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$ at 250-280 GPa starting from the (La,Sc$_2$) alloy are unsuccessful and indicate the absence of even traces of superconductivity at 245-300 K in all the resulting La-Sc-H hydrides. The method for preparing the precursor by simultaneous deposition of La and Sc metals may be a key factor for the successful synthesis of LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$.
summary: This work investigates the potential for room-temperature superconductivity in $	ext{LaSc}_2	ext{H}_{24}$ by combining advanced theoretical calculations with multiple high-pressure electrical measurements. While theory suggests high $T_c$ under optimal conditions, the experimental results from synthesizing La-Sc alloys fail to confirm this superconductivity near room temperature. The paper highlights the significant gap between theoretical prediction and practical material synthesis.
main_problem: The paper analyzes the theoretical feasibility and experimental reproducibility of room-temperature superconductivity in the high-pressure hydride $	ext{P6/mmm-LaSc}_2	ext{H}_{24}$. It contrasts theoretical predictions with results from high-pressure synthesis attempts.
main_result: While theory predicts high $T_c$ using specific methods, experimental synthesis attempts using La-Sc alloys failed to show superconductivity near room temperature, suggesting the material is difficult to realize or unstable under ambient synthesis conditions.
method: The study combines theoretical calculations (Eliashberg function analysis, stability analysis using $\xi$) with multiple high-pressure electrical transport measurements using Diamond Anvil Cells (DACs).
model_or_system: The physical system is the lanthanum-scandium hydride $	ext{LaSc}_2	ext{H}_{24}$ under extreme pressure (200-300 GPa). Theoretical modeling involves electron-phonon coupling calculations, while experiments use $	ext{La-Sc}$ alloy precursors reacted with hydrogen sources.
key_observables: Superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$), electrical resistance $R(T)$, stability parameter ($\xi$), and electron-phonon coupling constant ($\lambda$).
important_parameters: Pressure (220-300 GPa), Temperature (230-300 K), and the electron-phonon coupling broadening parameter ($\sigma$).
main_assumptions: Theoretical calculations rely on approximations like the tetrahedral method or Gaussian broadening of the delta function; experimental results are limited by the synthesis method and measurement window.
figures_summary: Figures show stability analysis ($\xi$) and resistance measurements ($R$ vs $T$) under DAC conditions, detailing both superconducting transitions and metallic behavior in synthesized La-Sc-H phases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from theoretical analysis of $T_c$ dependence on calculation parameters ($\sigma$) and stability ($\xi$), to comparing theoretical predictions with multiple high-pressure electrical measurements performed on synthesized La-Sc-H samples.
why_interesting: The interplay between complex theoretical models (like electron-phonon coupling) and challenging high-pressure experimental realization makes this relevant for understanding strongly correlated or exotic superconducting states in condensed matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Qualitative mechanism of possible instability of the metallic hydrogen sublattice in superhydrides during decompression. (A) Experimental picture of hydride decompression with an increase in the critical temperature, sample resistance, electron-phonon interaction parameter, and, eventually, disappearance of superconductivity. The color scale corresponds to the transition from low (blue) to high (red) values of normal state and residual resistance, Tc, λ upon going from high pressures (high P) to low pressures (low P).
